audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In this section, you will hear two overseas students, Kate and Luki, being interviewed by a university counselor.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to the example will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, there, Kate.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Come on in.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: How are you today?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Fine, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Hi, Luki.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: How's things?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Well, as I explained on the phone, I'm a counselor here at the Student Services section of the university.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: And I'm interviewing overseas students to help me draw up a guide for new students.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: So, I'd be grateful if you could tell me a little about your time since you've been here in Cambridge.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Now, Kate, let's start with you.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: OK, um, this is your 2nd semester, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Could you tell us something about your first impressions of the town when you arrived?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Uh. Yeah, well, first of all, I was struck by how quiet it is here in the evening.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Kate's first impression of the town is that it is quiet in the evening.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, quiet is the answer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen to the first interview with Kate, and complete the notes by filling in the numbered spaces 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, there, Kate.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Come on in.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: How are you today?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Fine, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Hi, Luki.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: How's things?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Well, as I explained on the phone, I'm a counselor here at the Student Services section of the university.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: And I'm interviewing overseas students to help me draw up a guide for new students.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: So, I'd be grateful if you could tell me a little about your time since you've been here in Cambridge.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Now, Kate, let's start with you.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: OK, um, this is your 2nd semester, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Could you tell us something about your first impressions of the town when you arrived?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Uh. Yeah, well, first of all I was struck by how quiet it is here in the evening.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Yeah, I suppose Cambridge is a quiet place.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Um.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Where did you live when you first arrived?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Well, I went straight into student accommodation.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: It was a kind of student hostel.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: All right, so, uh, you didn't have to worry about doing your own cooking or anything like that.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: But sometimes I wished I had, the food at the hostel was awful.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Oh, dear.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: But how were the other students?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Ha, to be honest, I haven't managed to make many friends, even though the place is full.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: People seem to keep to themselves.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: They're not really very friendly.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Oh, I'm sorry to hear that.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Um. Well, what about the actual course?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: You're studying... um...
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: I'm doing a master's by course work in environmental studies.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: All right, and how are you finding that?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Yeah, well, it's been pretty good really.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: I've enjoyed the course, but I feel there hasn't been enough contact with the lecturers.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Uh. They all seem to be incredibly busy.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: The only chance I've really had to talk to them was on the field trip.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Well, that's no good.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Uh huh. Um, could anything be done to improve the course in your opinion?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Well.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: I think it would be helpful to have meetings with lecturers on the course, say once a fortnight - something like that.
audioscript.speaker: Kate

audioscript.text: Regular meetings.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Yes, that could certainly help.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Now Kate we'll come back to you in a minute, but I'd just like to ask Luki some questions.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Before they continue the interview, look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you listen to the rest of the interview, complete the notes.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Luki, where are you from?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: I am from Indonesia.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: And how did you find Cambridge when you first arrived?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Well, I like it here.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: I think the city is very beautiful.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: What about your accommodation?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Was that OK?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Yes, OK.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: At first, I stayed with a family for 3 months.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: They were very kind to me, but they had three young children, and I found it difficult to study.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Right, I see.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: So, after 3 months, I moved out, and now I live with two other students in a student house.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: It's much cheaper, and we like it there.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: And then, what about your studies?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: What are you studying?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: I'm doing a Bachelor of Computing.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Computing, I see.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Um, apart from the language difficulties,
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: if you can separate them, how have you found the course?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: OK, but...
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Yes, go on.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Well the main difficulty for me is getting time on the computers in the computer room.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: It's always busy, and this makes it very hard to do my practical work.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: I'm sure it would.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Can you reserve time in the computer room?
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: No, you can't.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: But it would certainly help if we could reserve computer time.
audioscript.speaker: Luki

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: I'll look into that and see if something can be done to improve things over there.
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: Now, let's go back to Kate...
audioscript.speaker: Counsellor

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Buying a Bicycle: Tips and Choices

audioscript.text: Section 2, now listen to the first part of the talk, and complete the notes by filling in the numbered spaces 11 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, last week we talked about buying camping equipment, and today I'd like to talk to you about buying a bicycle.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: A simple enough exercise you might imagine, but there are lots of things to look out for to make sure you get the best deal for your money.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Well, the range of bicycles is enormous.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: There are racing bikes, touring bikes, mountain bikes, or just plain ordinary bikes for riding round town.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: They vary enormously in two basic ways, price and quality.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: This means that the choice you make will probably be determined by the amount of money you want to pay, your own personal needs, what is actually available, or a compromise of all three things.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: However, in broad terms, you can spend anything from $50 to $2,000 on a bike.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: So you'll need to know what you're looking for.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Single speed cycles, that is bikes with no gears, are really only suited to short casual rides.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Their attraction is their simplicity and reliability.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: After years of neglect, they still manage to function, though not always too efficiently.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: If it's basic transport you're after, then you can't go wrong.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: 3 speed cycles on the other hand are all that is really necessary for most town riding, going to the shops and things like that.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Like the single speed bike, they're simple and reliable.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: If you are going to be going up and down lots of hills, then you'll probably want something more efficient.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: 5 and 10 speed bicycles are best suited to riding over long distances or hilly terrain, and to serious touring.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: So, if it's serious touring you're interested in, get a 5 or 10 speed bike.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: However, it's worth remembering that the difference in price between a 5 and 10 speed cycle is usually very little, and so it's well worth paying that little bit extra to get the 10 speed one.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: So I would tend to recommend the 10 speed bike as the price is similar.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: However, you'll be getting better quality components.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Now the next thing we need to look at is size.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Buying a cycle is like buying clothes, first of all you find the right size, and then you try it on to see if it fits.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Contrary to what you might imagine, the size of the cycle is not determined by the size of the wheels - except in children's cycles - but by the size of the frame.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: So you'll need to measure the length of your legs and arms to get a frame that is the right size for you.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: Well, that's all from Helpful Hints for today.
audioscript.speaker: Radio presenter

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: Radio presenter

utterances.text: However, you'll be getting better quality components.
utterances.speaker: Radio presenter

utterances.text: Now the next thing we need to look at is size.
utterances.speaker: Radio presenter

utterances.text: Buying a cycle is like buying clothes, first of all you find the right size, and then you try it on to see if it fits.
utterances.speaker: Radio presenter

utterances.text: Contrary to what you might imagine, the size of the cycle is not determined by the size of the wheels - except in children's cycles - but by the size of the frame.
utterances.speaker: Radio presenter

utterances.text: So you'll need to measure the length of your legs and arms to get a frame that is the right size for you.
utterances.speaker: Radio presenter

utterances.text: Well, that's all from Helpful Hints for today.
utterances.speaker: Radio presenter

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS OR A NUMBER

items.title: There are many kinds of bicycles available:

items: Single speed cycles are suitable for <input>.
Three speed cycles are suitable for <input>.
Five and ten speed cycles are suitable for longer distances, hills and <input>.
Ten speed bikes are better because they are <input> price but <input>.
The size of the bicycle is determined by the size of the <input>.

test_text.title: The History and Growth of Bananas in Australia

audioscript.text: Section 3, in this section, you will hear a conversation between two students, Fiona and Martin, talking about a tutorial topic.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi there, Martin.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: How are you going with your Australian studies tutorial paper?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Oh, good.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: I've finished it actually.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Lucky you.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: What did you do it on?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: I'm still trying to find an interesting topic.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Well, after some consideration, I decided to look at the history of banana growing in Australia.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: (surprised) Banana growing!
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Yes, banana growing.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: (sarcastically) Fascinating, I'm sure.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Well...
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Not as boring as you'd think. And I wanted to tie it in to the work I've been doing on primary industries and the economy.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Anyway, I bet there are a few things you didn't know about bananas!
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Such as?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Such as the fact that bananas were among the first plants ever to be domesticated.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Oh, really?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Yes, they're an extremely nourishing food.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: I suppose you're going to tell me the whole history of banana growing now, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Well...
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: It'll be a good practice run for my tutorial next week.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: I'll do the same for you sometime.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: OK. Fire away.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: So where were these bananas first domesticated?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: According to my research, the Cavendish banana, which is a type of banana, and the first type to be cultivated here, actually originated in China, but they had a fairly roundabout route before they got to Australia.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: You mean they didn't go straight from China to Australia?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: No, they didn't.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: It seems that in 1826 bananas were taken from South China to England.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: I suppose they would have made a welcome addition to the English diet.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm sure.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Well, apparently there was an English Duke who was particularly fond of bananas,
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: and he used to cultivate them in his hothouse, which is where you have to grow them in England, of course, because of the cool climate,
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: and they became quite popular in the UK.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: So, he was the one responsible for cultivating the Cavendish banana, which was then introduced into Australia.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: I see, and we've been growing them ever since.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Now look at questions 25 to 32.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As the conversation continues, complete Martin's notes.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Are they hard to grow?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Well, yes and no.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: To grow them in your garden, no, not really, but to grow them commercially, you need to know what you're doing.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: You see, you only get one bunch of bananas per tree, and it can take up to 3 years for a tree to bear fruit, if you don't do anything special to it.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: But this period is greatly reduced with modern growing methods.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Particularly in plantations where you have perfect tropical conditions.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: So what are you looking at?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: One year, two years?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: No, no, around 15 months in good conditions for a tree to produce a bunch of bananas.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: And once you've got your bunch, you cut the bunch and the plant down.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: So how do the trees reproduce then?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Well bananas are normally grown from suckers which spring up around the parent plant, usually just above the plant.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: They tend to like to grow uphill, at least, that's the common wisdom.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: So, that's why banana plantations are usually on hillsides, is it?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Yeah, they grow best like that.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: That's interesting.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: If you plant them in rich soil and give them plenty of water at the beginning of summer, then they should be well advanced by the beginning of winter, when growth virtually stops.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: But in a country like England, they're hard to grow,
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: although you can grow them in a hothouse.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: But in Australia, it's not difficult?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: No, though, even here, the growers put plastic bags around the bunches to protect them and keep them warm.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: If you go up to the banana growing districts, you'll see all these banana trees with plastic bags on them.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: But how do they stop the bananas going bad before they reach the shops?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Well, the banana bunches are picked well before the fruit is ripe.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Once you cut the bunch, the bananas stop growing, but they do continue to ripen.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: The interesting thing is that once one banana ripens, it gives off a gas which then helps all the others to ripen.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: So they pretty much all ripen within a few hours of each other.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Amazing.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: So do we export lots of bananas overseas to Europe and Asia for instance?
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Well, oddly enough, no.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: I believe New Zealand takes a small proportion of the crop, but otherwise they're mostly grown for the domestic market, which is surprising when you think about it, because we grow an enormous number of bananas each year.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: Yes, well, thank you for all that information.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: I'm sure the tutorial paper will go really well.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: You certainly seem to have done your research on the subject.
audioscript.speaker: Fiona

audioscript.text: Let's hope so.
audioscript.speaker: Martin

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Healthy Eating on a Student Budget

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You are going to hear part of a public lecture for new students.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, look at questions 33 to 35.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 33 to 35.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Uh. Good morning. Good morning everyone. And welcome to our regular lecture on health issues.
audioscript.speaker: John

audioscript.text: Um. The series of lectures is organized by the Students' union, and is part of the union's attempt to help you.
audioscript.speaker: John

audioscript.text: The students of this university to stay healthy while coping with study and social life at the same time.
audioscript.speaker: John

audioscript.text: So it's a great pleasure for me to welcome back Ms Diane Greenbaum.
audioscript.speaker: John

audioscript.text: Who is a professional dietician, and who has been kind enough to give up her time, in what I know is a very hectic schedule, to come along and talk to us today.
audioscript.speaker: John

audioscript.text: Thank you, thank you very much, John.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: May I say it's a pleasure to be back.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Now stresses at university, being away from home and having to look after yourselves, uh, learning your way around the campus.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: All contribute to making it quite hard sometimes to ensure that your diet is adequate.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: So today, I'm going to talk about ways of making sure that you eat well, while at the same time staying within your budget.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Now look at questions 36 to 41.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As you listen to the second part of the lecture, complete the notes and diagram in the spaces provided.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: If you have a well balanced diet, then you should be getting all the vitamins that you need for normal daily living.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: However, sometimes we think we're eating the right foods, but the vitamins are escaping.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Perhaps as a result of cooking, and anyway we're not getting the full benefit of them.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Now if you lack vitamins in any way, the solution isn't to rush off and take vitamin pills, though they they can sometimes help.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: No, it's far better to look at your diet and how you prepare your food.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: So, what are vitamins?
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Well, the dictionary tells us they are "food factors essential in small quantities to maintain life".
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Now, there are fat soluble vitamins which can be stored for quite some time by the body.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: And there are water soluble vitamins which are removed more rapidly from the body, and so a regular daily intake of these ones is needed.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: OK, so how can you ensure that your diet contains enough of the vitamins you need?
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Well, first of all, you may have to establish some new eating habits.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: No more chips at the uni canteen, I'm afraid.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Now firstly, you must eat a variety of foods.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Then you need to ensure that you eat at least 4 servings of fruit and vegetables daily.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Now you'll need to shop two or three times a week to make sure that they're fresh, and store your vegetables in the fridge or in a cool dark place.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Now, let's just refresh our memories by looking at the healthy diet pyramid.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: OK, can you all see that?
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Hmm. Yep.
audioscript.speaker: John

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Well now as you see, we've got three levels to our pyramid, at the top, in the smallest area.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Are the things which we should really be trying to avoid as much as possible, things like yes, sugar, salt, butter, all that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Uh. Next on the middle of our pyramid, we find the things that we can eat in moderation, uh, not too much though, and that's where we find milk, lean meat, fish, nuts, eggs.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: And then at the bottom of the pyramid are the things that you can eat lots of, because they're the things that are really good for you.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: Uh. Here we have bread, vegetables, and fruit.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: So, don't lose sight of your healthy diet pyramid when you do your shopping.
audioscript.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: No more chips at the uni canteen, I'm afraid.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Now firstly, you must eat a variety of foods.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Then you need to ensure that you eat at least 4 servings of fruit and vegetables daily.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Now you'll need to shop two or three times a week to make sure that they're fresh, and store your vegetables in the fridge or in a cool dark place.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Now, let's just refresh our memories by looking at the healthy diet pyramid.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: OK, can you all see that?
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Hmm. Yep.
utterances.speaker: John

utterances.text: Good.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Well now as you see, we've got three levels to our pyramid, at the top, in the smallest area.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Are the things which we should really be trying to avoid as much as possible, things like yes, sugar, salt, butter, all that sort of thing.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Uh. Next on the middle of our pyramid, we find the things that we can eat in moderation, uh, not too much though, and that's where we find milk, lean meat, fish, nuts, eggs.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: And then at the bottom of the pyramid are the things that you can eat lots of, because they're the things that are really good for you.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: Uh. Here we have bread, vegetables, and fruit.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: So, don't lose sight of your healthy diet pyramid when you do your shopping.
utterances.speaker: Diane Greenbaum

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

(b) Water soluble vitamins - not stored, so you need a <input>.
